menstrual.text: Iron-rich foods like spinach, lentils, and beetroot restore energy during your period.
Gentle yoga or stretching eases cramps and bloating without straining your body.
Heat therapy — a warm pad on your lower abdomen — naturally reduces uterine cramping.
Staying well hydrated reduces bloating and fatigue; aim for an extra glass or two today.
Dark chocolate (70%+) is rich in magnesium, which directly eases menstrual cramps.
Omega-3 fatty acids in flaxseed and fatty fish reduce the inflammation behind period pain.
Avoiding caffeine during heavy days can reduce vascular cramping for many women.
Raspberry leaf tea is a traditional uterine tonic — a warm cup can take the edge off cramps.
A slow walk outdoors boosts endorphins without taxing your body during menstruation.
Rest is productive right now. Your uterus is doing real physical work — honour that.
Vitamin B1 (thiamine) found in whole grains and legumes may reduce dysmenorrhoea severity.
Chamomile tea has antispasmodic properties that help relax uterine muscle contractions.
Limiting high-sodium foods like packaged snacks during your period reduces water retention.
Warm soups and stews are gentle on digestion and comforting when energy is low.
Be kind to yourself today. Your energy is at its cyclical low — and that is entirely normal.

follicular.text: Oestrogen is rising — a great time to take on new projects, challenges, and decisions.
Your metabolism is running efficiently now; lighter meals tend to feel more satisfying.
Strength training is most effective during the follicular phase thanks to peak oestrogen.
Fermented foods like yoghurt, kefir, and kimchi support gut health as your energy climbs.
Social connections feel easier this week — reach out to someone you have been meaning to call.
Flax and pumpkin seeds contain lignans that gently support healthy oestrogen levels.
This is the best phase to start a new habit — motivation and focus are at their sharpest.
Sprouts and raw salads are easy to digest and pack maximum nutrients in the follicular phase.
Lean proteins — eggs, paneer, legumes — support muscle building when oestrogen is high.
Your pain tolerance is higher now — a good window for dental or other medical appointments.
Creative energy flows more freely during the follicular phase. Start that project you have delayed.
Cardio and HIIT feel natural and energising this week — your body is ready for it.
Green vegetables and vitamin C foods help your liver clear oestrogen efficiently.
Plan important presentations or difficult conversations now — you are at your most persuasive.
Oestrogen boosts collagen production, so your skin is likely at its clearest right now.

ovulation.text: Your communication and social skills are at their cyclical peak. Important conversations go well today.
Fertility is highest in a 12-24 hour window around ovulation — roughly plus or minus 2 days from today.
Energy and confidence tend to peak at ovulation. Channel this into something that matters to you.
Anti-inflammatory foods — berries, turmeric, leafy greens — support a smooth ovulation.
Your body is doing something remarkable this week. Take a moment to appreciate it.
Subtle shifts in your voice and posture make you more expressive and engaging around ovulation.
A natural rise in libido around ovulation is healthy, normal, and hormonally driven.
Sunflower and sesame seeds support progesterone production in the second half of your cycle.
High-intensity workouts like HIIT or running feel easiest and most rewarding around ovulation.
Basal body temperature rises slightly after ovulation — tracking this confirms the timing.
Zinc in pumpkin seeds, lentils, and chickpeas supports healthy egg development and ovulation.
Your peak collaborative energy makes this an ideal time for teamwork and leadership.
Broccoli and cruciferous vegetables help your liver metabolise the surge in oestrogen.
Light, colourful meals align well with your body's heightened metabolic energy this week.
Each cycle is your body's way of beginning again. Celebrate that resilience today.

luteal.text: Magnesium-rich foods like dark chocolate, almonds, and spinach directly ease PMS symptoms.
Progesterone is rising now — you may feel calmer but also more inward-looking. Both are valid.
Complex carbohydrates like oats, sweet potato, and brown rice stabilise the serotonin dips linked to PMS.
Gentle exercise like pilates, swimming, or yoga maintains endorphins without overstressing your body.
Luteal-phase cravings are real and hormonal. Honour them mindfully rather than suppressing them.
Calcium-rich foods — dairy, sesame, leafy greens — have been shown to reduce PMS severity.
Vitamin B6 in bananas, chickpeas, and sunflower seeds supports serotonin production this week.
Journaling and creative solitude feel deeply rewarding during this inward phase of your cycle.
Alcohol amplifies progesterone-related mood shifts and disrupts sleep — less is more this week.
Evening primrose oil may ease breast tenderness and mood swings for some women in the luteal phase.
Limiting refined sugar stabilises blood-sugar swings that worsen PMS irritability and fatigue.
Chamomile or valerian tea before bed improves sleep quality as progesterone peaks.
Intuition tends to be sharper in the luteal phase. Trust those gut feelings — they are worth examining.
Slow yoga and stretching release the cortisol buildup that accumulates in the late luteal phase.
Plan low-key commitments this week. Your energy naturally turns inward — and that is a gift.
